title: Core Concepts
text: rxsim organises clinical trial simulation around three collaborating objects. Population owns subject-level data tracks subject’s enrollment dropout times. Timer drives trial clock. stores discrete timepoints per arm evaluates condition-triggered analyses data meet criterion. Trial orchestrates simulation iterating timepoints, updating populations, snapshotting enrolled cohort, collecting results. run() loop Trial executes timepoint follows sequence: workflows never construct objects hand. Instead use high-level entry point replicate_trial() + run_trials(), build execute n independent Trial objects generator functions. Understanding three classes directly useful want : inspect locked snapshot mid-simulation debugging write custom multi-timepoint designs gen_plan() express use Trial$new() directly one-single-run simulation (Example 8) rest vignette unpacks building block turn.

title: Core Concepts
text: Population object represents single arm’s worth subjects. stores endpoint data alongside two tracking vectors (enrolled, dropped) record calendar time subject enrolled dropped — NA neither event occurred yet.

title: Core Concepts
text: data field must data frame least four columns: readout_time column calendar time — lag subject’s enrollment date date endpoint observed. Use 0 immediately available data (e.g., baseline measure) positive value delayed readouts (e.g., readout_time = 12 means endpoint read 12 weeks enrollment).

title: Core Concepts
text: common case single continuous endpoint, vector_to_dataframe() wraps numeric vector required format columns id, data, readout_time = 0: can also build data frame — useful covariates, time-varying endpoints, different readout lags per subject.

title: Core Concepts
text: subject contributes multiple measurements (longitudinal pharmacokinetic design), measurement appears row distinct readout_time. Population detects automatically stores number rows per subject n_readouts:
code: # Two timepoints per subject: baseline (0) and week 12 (12) long_df <- data.frame(   id           = rep(1:4, each = 2),   readout_time = rep(c(0, 12), times = 4),   response     = rnorm(8) )  pop_long <- Population$new(name = "treatment", data = long_df) pop_long$n          # 4 unique subjects #> [1] 4 pop_long$n_readouts # 2 rows per subject #> [1] 2

title: Core Concepts
text: enrolled dropped numeric vectors length n, initialised NA every subject. NA means “yet acted ”: subject exists pool enrolled dropped. Trial assigns calendar times vectors simulation clock advances. set_enrolled(n, time) marks n randomly chosen currently unenrolled subjects enrolled time. set_dropped(n, time) similarly marks n randomly chosen enrolled---yet-dropped subjects dropped time. Trial’s run() method calls setters automatically based Timer’s schedule — rarely need call directly.
code: set.seed(1) pop <- Population$new(name = "control", data = vector_to_dataframe(rnorm(8))) pop$enrolled  # all NA to start #> [1] NA NA NA NA NA NA NA NA  pop$set_enrolled(5, time = 2) pop$enrolled  # 5 subjects enrolled at t=2, 3 still NA #> [1]  2  2  2 NA NA  2 NA  2  pop$set_dropped(2, time = 7) pop$dropped   # 2 of the enrolled subjects dropped at t=7 #> [1]  7 NA NA NA NA NA NA  7

title: Core Concepts
text: Timer drives trial clock. holds two data structures: timelist specifies many subjects enroll drop arm time unit, conditions list defines analysis-triggering criteria. unique time timelist, Trial$run() asks Timer evaluate conditions current snapshot.

title: Core Concepts
text: entry timelist four fields: Timepoints per-arm two arms trial may enroll subjects different schedules, enrollment events one arm affect . Rather adding timepoints one one, add_timepoints() accepts data frame four columns — exactly gen_plan() gen_timepoints() return. See Enrollment & Dropout Modeling details.
code: t <- Timer$new(name = "my_timer")  t$add_timepoint(time = 1,  arm = "control",   enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) t$add_timepoint(time = 1,  arm = "treatment", enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) t$add_timepoint(time = 2,  arm = "control",   enroller = 3L, dropper = 1L) t$add_timepoint(time = 2,  arm = "treatment", enroller = 4L, dropper = 0L) t$add_timepoint(time = 10, arm = "control",   enroller = 0L, dropper = 0L) t$add_timepoint(time = 10, arm = "treatment", enroller = 0L, dropper = 0L)  t$get_unique_times()   # c(1, 2, 10) #> [1]  1  2 10 t$get_end_timepoint()  # 10 #> [1] 10 t$get_n_arms()         # 2 #> [1] 2

title: Core Concepts
text: condition pairs filter expression optional analysis function. Trial calls check_conditions() timepoint, condition’s expression evaluated current snapshot using dplyr::filter(). filtered result non-empty (condition satisfied), associated analysis function called return value stored. Conditions added add_condition(). ... arguments dplyr::filter-style boolean expressions, evaluated lazily snapshot columns: analysis function provided, check_conditions() returns filtered subset instead calling function — convenient inspection debugging.
code: # A toy snapshot data frame snapshot <- data.frame(   id          = 1:8,   arm         = rep(c("control", "treatment"), 4),   enroll_time = c(1, 1, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA, NA),   data        = rnorm(8) )  t2 <- Timer$new(name = "demo")  # Fire when at least 4 subjects are enrolled t2$add_condition(   sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= 4,   analysis = function(df, current_time) {     data.frame(n_enrolled = sum(!is.na(df$enroll_time)),                fired_at   = current_time)   },   name = "interim" )  res <- t2$check_conditions(locked_data = snapshot, current_time = 5) res[["interim"]] #>   n_enrolled fired_at #> 1          4        5

title: Core Concepts
text: Trial wires one Populations Timer together runs simulation.

title: Core Concepts
text: population argument list Population objects, one per arm. arm labels Population’s name field must match arm identifiers Timer’s timelist.
code: Trial$new(   name       = "my_trial",   seed       = 42,           # optional; set for reproducibility   timer      = my_timer,   population = list(pop_a, pop_b) )

title: Core Concepts
text: Calling trial$run() executes following loop: Iterate unique time t Timer’s timelist, ascending order. arm, apply set_enrolled() set_dropped() according arm’s timepoint entry. Build combined snapshot row-binding arms’ enrolled subjects. Four columns appended Trial: Call timer$check_conditions(snapshot, t) — evaluate conditions collect analysis results. condition fired, store snapshot locked_data[["time_t"]] analysis outputs results[["time_t"]].

title: Core Concepts
text: locked_data named list snapshots, one per unique timepoint least one analysis fired. snapshot full subject-level data frame moment — useful auditing subjects enrolled, computing post-hoc statistics, debugging analysis function. results time-indexed structure, element named list — one entry per condition fired timepoint. value whatever analysis function returned.

title: Core Concepts
code: set.seed(7)  # Two small populations pop_a <- Population$new("A", data = vector_to_dataframe(rnorm(10))) pop_b <- Population$new("B", data = vector_to_dataframe(rnorm(10, mean = 0.5)))  # Timer: enroll in two waves, final readout at time 15 tm <- Timer$new("tm") tm$add_timepoint(time = 1,  arm = "A", enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) tm$add_timepoint(time = 1,  arm = "B", enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) tm$add_timepoint(time = 3,  arm = "A", enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) tm$add_timepoint(time = 3,  arm = "B", enroller = 5L, dropper = 0L) tm$add_timepoint(time = 15, arm = "A", enroller = 0L, dropper = 0L) tm$add_timepoint(time = 15, arm = "B", enroller = 0L, dropper = 0L)  # Trigger: fire at calendar time 15 trigger_by_calendar(15, tm, analysis = function(df, current_time) {   enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))   data.frame(     n       = nrow(enrolled),     mean_A  = mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "A"]),     mean_B  = mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "B"])   ) })  trial <- Trial$new(name = "demo", seed = 7, timer = tm,                    population = list(pop_a, pop_b)) trial$run()  # Inspect results prettify_results(trial$results) #>   time cal_time_15.n cal_time_15.mean_A cal_time_15.mean_B #> 1   15            20          0.1039757           1.282517 # How many subjects were enrolled at each timepoint? sapply(trial$locked_data, function(snap) sum(!is.na(snap$enroll_time))) #> time_15  #>      20

title: Core Concepts
text: Triggers mechanism rxsim knows analyse data compute. Understanding stored evaluated key writing correct flexible simulation code.

title: Core Concepts
text: trigger condition needs stored expression, immediately evaluated one. write sum(!.na(enroll_time)) >= 20, R evaluate point definition — data exists. rlang::exprs() wraps expression quoted form evaluated later, inside check_conditions(), actual snapshot:
code: # This is a stored expression, not an evaluated boolean: rlang::exprs(sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= 20)

title: Core Concepts
text: want inject value current R environment stored expression, use !!. Without , variable name treated column name snapshot data frame — almost certainly want: distinction matters loop scenarios different sample sizes: iteration bake target_n value via !!.
code: target_n <- 40  # CORRECT: !! injects the value 40 at definition time trigger <- rlang::exprs(sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!target_n)  # WRONG: "target_n" would be looked for as a column name in the snapshot trigger_bad <- rlang::exprs(sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= target_n)

what: Columns available in a trigger expression
title: Core Concepts
text: trigger expression evaluated snapshot data frame, contains columns Population’s data plus four columns appended Trial: enroll_time — calendar enrollment time (NA enrolled) drop_time — calendar dropout time (NA dropped) measurement_time — enroll_time + readout_time time — current clock time arm — arm label user-defined endpoint column (e.g., data, response, tte) also available.

title: Core Concepts
text: two helpers wrap common trigger patterns: trigger_by_calendar(cal_time, timer, analysis) fires specific calendar time — useful pre-planned final analysis scheduled interim review: trigger_by_fraction(fraction, timer, sample_size, analysis) fires given fraction total target sample enrolled — natural information-fraction-based interim rules:
code: trigger_by_calendar(24, timer = tm, analysis = function(df, current_time) {   data.frame(n_enrolled = sum(!is.na(df$enroll_time))) }) # Interim at 50% enrollment trigger_by_fraction(0.5, timer = tm, sample_size = 100,                     analysis = function(df, current_time) {                       enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))                       data.frame(n = nrow(enrolled), time = current_time)                     })

title: Core Concepts
text: can condition expression involving snapshot columns. time--event endpoints commonly means waiting target number events rather target enrollment count: Multiple conditions can combined commas (ANDed together, just dplyr::filter()):
code: # Fire when 30 events have been observed (event = 1, censored = 0) timer$add_condition(   sum(event == 1 & !is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!n_events,   analysis = my_tte_analysis,   name = "event_driven_interim" ) # Fire only for the treatment arm when 15 subjects are enrolled there timer$add_condition(   arm == "treatment",   sum(!is.na(enroll_time[arm == "treatment"])) >= 15,   analysis = my_analysis,   name = "trt_interim" )

title: Core Concepts
text: Every analysis function receives two arguments: df: full snapshot — arms, currently enrolled subjects. condition filter determined whether fire; analysis function decides compute full data. current_time: numeric clock time condition fired.
code: my_analysis <- function(df, current_time) {   enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))   data.frame(     fired_at  = current_time,     n_ctrl    = sum(enrolled$arm == "control"),     n_trt     = sum(enrolled$arm == "treatment"),     mean_diff = mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "treatment"]) -                 mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "control"])   ) }

title: Core Concepts
text: analysis function’s return value stored verbatim results. collect_results() handles three cases: data.frame (recommended) — standard pattern; one row per trigger event convention. named list — coerced single-row data frame. NULL — silently skipped collect_results(). Returning NULL useful side-effects-analyses (e.g., writing log entry) marking trigger condition yet satisfied.

what: Putting it together: replicate_trial() and run_trials()
title: Core Concepts
text: Building single Trial hand useful exploration, purpose rxsim operating-characteristic simulation across many replicates. replicate_trial() + run_trials() handle ergonomically.

title: Core Concepts
text: n replicates, replicate_trial(): Calls gen_plan() enrollment dropout functions generate fresh, stochastic enrollment/dropout schedule. Builds new Timer, loads schedule via add_timepoints(), registers analysis triggers. Calls population generator function draw fresh subject-level endpoint data. Constructs Trial$new() wiring new Timer Population objects together. replicate therefore independent endpoint data independent enrollment timing — sources variability operating characteristic simulations designed characterise.

title: Enrollment & Dropout Modeling
text: Enrollment pace determines subject enters trial therefore trial clock can reach timepoints trigger analyses. real programs, rate sites activate patients screened uncertain — enrollment random process. randomness ignored (example, assuming subjects enroll fixed schedule), simulated distribution study duration artificially narrow, operating characteristics power expected decision error rates can materially overestimated. Capturing stochastic enrollment propagates uncertainty study duration every downstream quantity simulation designed evaluate.

title: Enrollment & Dropout Modeling
text: rxsim offers two complementary strategies, suited different modeling philosophy: gen_plan() — stochastic. supply random function inter-enrollment times. Every call draws new realization, replicate gets unique enrollment timeline. Use trial-duration variability substantively important want operating characteristics reflect . gen_timepoints() — piecewise-constant. specify many subjects enroll per unit time time period. schedule fixed deterministic. Every replicate uses exactly enrollment pattern. Use well-characterized enrollment plan want isolate variability endpoints analyses variability timing. functions return data frame passed directly add_timepoints() populate Timer.

code: set.seed(42)  population_generators <- list(   pbo = function(n) data.frame(     id = seq_len(n), response = rnorm(n, 0.0), readout_time = 0   ),   trt = function(n) data.frame(     id = seq_len(n), response = rnorm(n, 0.5), readout_time = 0   ) )  analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= 12L),     analysis = function(df, current_time) {       enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))       data.frame(         n         = nrow(enrolled),         mean_pbo  = mean(enrolled$response[enrolled$arm == "pbo"]),         mean_trt  = mean(enrolled$response[enrolled$arm == "trt"])       )     }   ) )  trials <- replicate_trial(   trial_name            = "demo",   sample_size           = 12,   arms                  = c("pbo", "trt"),   allocation            = c(1, 1),   enrollment            = function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1),   dropout               = function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.05),   analysis_generators   = analysis_generators,   population_generators = population_generators,   n                     = 3 )  results <- run_trials(trials)  # Collect results across replicates collect_results(trials) #>   replicate timepoint analysis  n   mean_pbo   mean_trt #> 1         1  9.501693    final 12  0.1510848  0.4637581 #> 2         2 19.250321    final 12 -0.2867542 -0.1167607 #> 3         3 12.210078    final 12 -0.5907032  0.6425829

what: Building a Timer manually with gen_timepoints()
title: Enrollment & Dropout Modeling
text: need fixed enrollment schedule want control replicate_trial() exposes, can assemble Timer directly. pattern : Call gen_timepoints() get schedule data frame. Create Timer$new(). Register timepoints add_timepoints(). Add analysis triggers (e.g., trigger_by_calendar()). Build populations construct Trial$new(). Use clone_trial() want multiple independent replicates sharing fixed schedule. every replicate cloned trial_fixed uses fixed_plan, enrollment timing constant — subject-level endpoint variability contributes simulation noise.
code: # Fixed enrollment schedule enroll_sched  <- list(end_time = c(4, 8, 12), rate = c(3, 6, 9)) dropout_sched <- list(end_time = c(4, 8, 12), rate = c(0, 1, 1))  fixed_plan <- gen_timepoints(   sample_size = 12,   arms        = c("pbo", "trt"),   allocation  = c(1, 1),   enrollment  = enroll_sched,   dropout     = dropout_sched )  tmr3 <- Timer$new(name = "fixed_timer") add_timepoints(tmr3, fixed_plan)  # Add an analysis at the final calendar time final_t <- tmr3$get_end_timepoint() trigger_by_calendar(   cal_time = final_t,   timer    = tmr3,   analysis = function(df, current_time) {     enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))     data.frame(n_enrolled = nrow(enrolled), time = current_time)   } )  # Build populations (sized to match the fixed plan) pop_pbo <- gen_population(   name        = "pbo",   generator   = function(n) data.frame(     id = seq_len(n), response = rnorm(n, 0), readout_time = 0   ),   sample_size = 6 ) pop_trt <- gen_population(   name        = "trt",   generator   = function(n) data.frame(     id = seq_len(n), response = rnorm(n, 0.5), readout_time = 0   ),   sample_size = 6 )  trial_fixed <- Trial$new(   name       = "fixed_schedule_trial",   timer      = tmr3,   seed       = 123,   population = list(pop_pbo, pop_trt) )  trial_fixed$run() prettify_results(trial_fixed$results) #>   time cal_time_5.n_enrolled cal_time_5.time #> 1    5                    12               5

title: Example 1: Two arm | Fixed design | Continuous
text: Capture scenario parameters. assume piece-wise linear enrollment. allocation = c(1, 1) specifies balanced randomisation, equal expected numbers per arm. Using tidyr::expand_grid() build scenario data.frame embeds design parameters directly analysis result row, making results self-documenting easy compare across parameter sweeps.
code: sample_size <- 100 arms <- c("pbo", "trt") allocation <- c(1,1) delta <- 0.2 enrollment_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1) dropout_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.01) scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation = list(allocation),   delta = delta )

title: Example 1: Two arm | Fixed design | Continuous

code: population_generators <- list(   pbo = function(n) data.frame(     id = 1:n,     value = rnorm(n),     readout_time = 1   ),   trt = function(n) data.frame(     id = 1:n,     value = rnorm(n, delta),     readout_time = 1   ) )

title: Example 1: Two arm | Fixed design | Continuous
text: want t-test sample_size subjects enrolled. trigger fires cumulative count enrolled subjects (sum(!.na(enroll_time))) reaches sample_size. Inside analysis function, subset(!.na(enroll_time)) drops subjects allocated yet enrolled. rxsim pre-generates full allocation list filtering essential. two-sample t-test compares endpoint values two enrolled arms.
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer){       df_enrolled <- df |> subset(!is.na(enroll_time))       tt <- t.test(value ~ arm, data = df_enrolled)       data.frame(         scenario,         n_total = nrow(df_enrolled),         mean_pbo = mean(df_enrolled$value[df_enrolled$arm == "pbo"]),         mean_trt = mean(df_enrolled$value[df_enrolled$arm == "trt"]),         p_value = unname(tt$p.value),         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 3: Two arm | Fixed design | Two correlated continuous endpoints | t-test
text: consider two-arm, fixed design two correlated continuous endpoints per subject. Enrollment piece-wise linear; analysis happens final time. rho = 0.60 represents moderate positive correlation two endpoints — plausible reflect underlying biological process. covariance matrix Sigma constructed marginal SDs correlation using standard identity cov(y1, y2) = rho × sd1 × sd2. Treatment shifts delta1 = 0.30 SD primary endpoint delta2 = 0.20 SD secondary, reflecting scenario primary endpoint sensitive treatment.
code: # Total target sample size sample_size <- 100  # Arms and allocation (balanced) arms       <- c("pbo", "trt") allocation <- c(1, 1)  # Piece-wise linear enrollment and dropout rates (per unit time) # (You can tweak these to match your program's cadence.) enrollment <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8, 12, 16),   rate     = c(5, 10, 15, 20) ) dropout <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8, 12, 16),   rate     = c(1, 2, 4, 8) )  # Endpoint model parameters # Endpoint correlation structure (common across arms for simplicity) rho  <- 0.60            # correlation between endpoint1 and endpoint2 sd1  <- 1.00            # SD of endpoint1 sd2  <- 1.00            # SD of endpoint2  # Mean structure per arm mu1_pbo <- 0.00         # Control mean for endpoint1 mu2_pbo <- 0.00         # Control mean for endpoint2  # Treatment effects (mean shifts) delta1  <- 0.30         # Treatment - control difference for endpoint1 delta2  <- 0.20         # Treatment - control difference for endpoint2  mu1_trt <- mu1_pbo + delta1 mu2_trt <- mu2_pbo + delta2  # Construct covariance matrix Sigma <- matrix(   c(sd1^2, rho*sd1*sd2,     rho*sd1*sd2, sd2^2),   nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE )  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation = list(allocation),   rho = rho,   delta1 = delta1,   delta2 = delta2 )

code: timepoints <- gen_timepoints(   sample_size = sample_size,   arms        = arms,   allocation  = allocation,   enrollment  = enrollment,   dropout     = dropout )  t <- Timer$new(name = "trial_timer_two_endpoints") add_timepoints(t, timepoints)  final_time <- t$get_end_timepoint() final_time #> [1] 12

title: Example 3: Two arm | Fixed design | Two correlated continuous endpoints | t-test
text: arm simulate two correlated endpoints per subject using MASS::mvrnorm. keep 50 subjects per arm (consistent sample_size = 100 equal allocation). MASS::mvrnorm draws n samples multivariate normal given mean vector covariance matrix Sigma. closure pattern (mk_population_generator) captures arm-specific means definition time, call population_generators$pbo(n) population_generators$trt(n) produces data correct arm parameters. Endpoint correlation flows entirely shared Sigma matrix — changing rho scenario automatically updates arms.
code: # Create closure to capture parameters mk_population_generator <- function(mu_y1, mu_y2) {   function(n) {     xy <- MASS::mvrnorm(       n     = n,       mu    = c(mu_y1, mu_y2),       Sigma = Sigma     )     data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       y1 = xy[, 1],       y2 = xy[, 2],       readout_time = 1     )   } }  population_generators <- list(   pbo = mk_population_generator(mu1_pbo, mu2_pbo),   trt = mk_population_generator(mu1_trt, mu2_trt) )

title: Example 3: Two arm | Fixed design | Two correlated continuous endpoints | t-test
text: Define enrollment/dropout profiles parameters. Note enrollment_fn dropout_fn stochastic functions passed replicate_trial(), uses gen_plan() generate per-replicate enrollment dropout times. separate piecewise-constant enrollment dropout lists used gen_timepoints() — lists drive deterministic timer, functions drive per-subject event times within simulated replicate.
code: arms <- c("pbo", "trt") allocation <- c(1, 1) enrollment_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1) dropout_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.01)

title: Example 3: Two arm | Fixed design | Two correlated continuous endpoints | t-test
text: fixed design: perform analysis . run two independent two-sample t-tests (one per endpoint). two t-tests run independently enrolled subset, producing unadjusted p-values p_y1 p_y2. p.adjust(..., method = "holm") applies Holm’s stepwise procedure: padj[1] corresponds endpoint smaller raw p-value (significant) padj[2] larger. adjusted values account familywise error rate across two endpoints, p_holm_y1 >= p_y1 always holds.
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       df_e <- df |> subset(!is.na(enroll_time))        p1 <- t.test(y1 ~ arm, data = df_e)$p.value       p2 <- t.test(y2 ~ arm, data = df_e)$p.value       padj <- p.adjust(c(p1, p2), method = "holm")        data.frame(         scenario,         p_y1      = unname(p1),         p_y2      = unname(p2),         p_holm_y1 = unname(padj[1]),         p_holm_y2 = unname(padj[2]),         n_total   = nrow(df_e),         n_pbo     = sum(df_e$arm == "pbo"),         n_trt     = sum(df_e$arm == "trt"),         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 4: Two arm | Fixed design | Correlated continuous & time-to-event endpoints | t-test + survival analysis
text: consider two-arm fixed design continuous endpoint (Y) time--event (TTE) endpoint per subject. endpoints correlated via shared Gaussian latent structure. Enrollment piece-wise linear; analysis happens final time. HR_trt = 0.70 encodes 30% hazard reduction treatment arm — clinically meaningful effect many oncology settings. lambda0 = 0.08 baseline event rate per time unit; combined n=1200, ensures substantial number TTE events even 3 replicates. sigma_frailty = 0.6 controls strength subject-level frailty term: larger values introduce -subject heterogeneity TTE, making frailty shared biological driver linking continuous TTE endpoints bivariate latent (Z1, Z2) rho = 0.50.
code: # Total target N (across both arms) sample_size <- 1200  # Arms and allocation (balanced) arms       <- c("pbo", "trt") allocation <- c(1, 1)  enrollment_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1) dropout_fn <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.01)  # Correlation between continuous endpoint driver (Z1) and frailty for TTE (Z2) rho <- 0.50  # Continuous endpoint parameters mu_pbo <- 0.0 delta  <- 0.3      # true treatment - placebo mean difference sd_y   <- 1.0  # TTE model parameters (Exponential PH with subject frailty exp(eta)) lambda0 <- 0.08    # baseline hazard (per time unit) HR_trt  <- 0.70    # treatment hazard ratio (<1 favors treatment)  # Frailty scale (eta = sigma_frailty * Z2) sigma_frailty <- 0.6  # Covariance of (Z1, Z2) SigmaZ <- matrix(c(1, rho, rho, 1), nrow = 2)  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation  = list(allocation),   delta       = delta,   rho         = rho,   hr_trt_true = HR_trt )

title: Example 4: Two arm | Fixed design | Correlated continuous & time-to-event endpoints | t-test + survival analysis
text: Per arm simulate subject-level latent bivariate normals (Z1, Z2) correlation rho. - continuous endpoint Y = mu_arm + sd_y * Z1. - TTE endpoint uses proportional-hazards exponential model subject-specific frailty: hazard_i = lambda_arm * exp(eta_i), eta_i = sigma_frailty * Z2 lambda_trt = lambda0 * HR_trt. Event time T_i ~ Exponential(rate = hazard_i). subject gets latent pair (Z1, Z2) drawn bivariate standard normal correlation rho. Z1 drives continuous endpoint: Y = mu_arm + sd_y * Z1. Z2 drives TTE multiplicative frailty: eta = sigma_frailty * Z2, hazard_i = lambda_arm * exp(eta_i), T_i ~ Exponential(hazard_i). shared latent structure means subjects higher Z2 (higher frailty, shorter TTE) also tend higher Z1 (higher Y), modelling biological scenario biomarker TTE share common underlying driver.
code: # Create generator function for arm-specific data mk_population_generator <- function(mu_y, lambda_arm) {   function(n) {     Z <- MASS::mvrnorm(n, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = SigmaZ)     Z1 <- Z[, 1]     Z2 <- Z[, 2]     y  <- mu_y + sd_y * Z1     eta <- sigma_frailty * Z2     rate_i <- lambda_arm * exp(eta)     tte_true <- rexp(n, rate = rate_i)      data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       y  = y,       tte_true = tte_true,       readout_time = 1     )   } }  lambda_pbo <- lambda0 lambda_trt <- lambda0 * HR_trt  population_generators <- list(   pbo = mk_population_generator(mu_y = mu_pbo, lambda_arm = lambda_pbo),   trt = mk_population_generator(mu_y = mu_pbo + delta, lambda_arm = lambda_trt) )

title: Example 4: Two arm | Fixed design | Correlated continuous & time-to-event endpoints | t-test + survival analysis
text: fixed design: analyze final timepoint. run two-sample t-test continuous endpoint log-rank test plus Cox PH TTE endpoint. censor_drop censors subject time elapsed enrollment dropout (infinity dropout). censor_admin provides administrative censoring: subject followed 100 time units last enrollment, common end--study rule. t_obs status capture actually observed. Surv() object passed survdiff() log-rank p-value coxph() Cox HR 95% CI. tryCatch guards edge cases (e.g., one arm events) simulation continues rather stopping error.
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       df_e <- df[!is.na(df$enroll_time), , drop = FALSE]       if (nrow(df_e) == 0) {         return(data.frame(scenario, note = "no enrolled subjects", stringsAsFactors = FALSE))       }        # Build observed TTE with censoring       censor_drop  <- ifelse(is.na(df_e$drop_time), Inf, pmax(0, df_e$drop_time - df_e$enroll_time))       censor_admin <- pmax(0, max(df_e$enroll_time) + 100 - df_e$enroll_time)        tte_true <- df_e$tte_true       t_obs    <- pmin(tte_true, censor_drop, censor_admin)       status   <- as.integer(tte_true <= pmin(censor_drop, censor_admin))        # Continuous endpoint t-test       p_t <- tryCatch(         t.test(y ~ arm, data = df_e)$p.value,         error = function(e) NA_real_       )        # Survival analysis: log-rank and Cox       surv_p <- NA_real_       hr <- hr_lo <- hr_hi <- NA_real_       events <- sum(status)        if (length(unique(df_e$arm)) == 2 && events > 0 && all(t_obs >= 0)) {         S <- Surv(time = t_obs, event = status)          surv_p <- tryCatch({           sd <- survdiff(S ~ arm, data = df_e)           1 - pchisq(sd$chisq, df = length(sd$n) - 1)         }, error = function(e) NA_real_)          cox <- tryCatch(coxph(S ~ arm, data = df_e), error = function(e) NULL)         if (!is.null(cox)) {           s <- summary(cox)           hr    <- unname(s$coef[1, "exp(coef)"])           hr_lo <- unname(s$conf.int[1, "lower .95"])           hr_hi <- unname(s$conf.int[1, "upper .95"])         }       }        data.frame(         scenario,         n_total    = nrow(df_e),         n_pbo      = sum(df_e$arm == "pbo"),         n_trt      = sum(df_e$arm == "trt"),         p_ttest_y  = p_t,         logrank_p  = surv_p,         hr_cox     = hr,         hr_ci_lo   = hr_lo,         hr_ci_hi   = hr_hi,         events     = events,         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 5: Multi-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Dunnett test + MCP-Mod
text: multi-arm, fixed design (placebo + 4 active doses) single continuous endpoint. final analysis, perform: Dunnett test: active doses vs placebo using normal-theory linear model. MCP-Mod: model-based multiple contrast test + model fitting candidate set dose–response shapes. Emax model generates mean responses via E(d) = e0 + emax × d / (ed50 + d), e0 = 0 (placebo baseline), emax = 1 (maximum achievable effect), ed50 = 20 (dose half-maximum effect). arm_names = paste0("d", doses) creates human-readable labels (d0, d5, d10, d20, d50) propagate trial data analysis results. delta = 0.1 minimum effect size clinical relevance used MCP-Mod, alpha = 0.05 controls family-wise Type error rate testing procedures.
code: # Dose levels (placebo + 4 actives) doses <- c(0, 5, 10, 20, 50)  # Total N and allocation (balanced across arms) sample_size <- 150 allocation  <- rep(1, length(doses)) arm_names   <- paste0("d", doses)  # e.g., d0, d5, ... used as arm labels  # Enrollment & dropout profile (piece-wise constant) enrollment <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8, 12, 16),   rate     = c(6, 12, 18, 24) ) dropout <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8, 12, 16),   rate     = c(0,  1,  2,  3) )  # Data-generating model: Emax with homoscedastic noise e0    <- 0.0 emax  <- 1.0 ed50  <- 20.0 sigma <- 1.0  mean_fun <- function(d) e0 + emax * d / (ed50 + d)  # operating characteristics alpha <- 0.05 delta <- 0.1  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation = list(allocation),   n_arms = length(doses),   alpha = alpha,   delta = delta )

code: timepoints <- gen_timepoints(   sample_size = sample_size,   arms        = arm_names,   allocation  = allocation,   enrollment  = enrollment,   dropout     = dropout )  # Timer tr_timer <- Timer$new(name = "timer_multiarm") add_timepoints(tr_timer, timepoints) final_time <- tr_timer$get_end_timepoint() final_time #> [1] 14

title: Example 5: Multi-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Dunnett test + MCP-Mod
text: mk_pop_gen closure factory: captures dose value d returns generator function , called n, draws n responses N(E(d), σ²). arm’s generator stores dose numeric column MCP-Mod requires actual dose values — just arm labels — fit dose-response models evaluate contrasts analysis time.
code: mk_pop_gen <- function(d) {   function(n) {     mu <- mean_fun(d)     y  <- rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma)     data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       dose = d,       y = y,       readout_time = 1     )   } }  population_generators <- lapply(seq_along(doses), function(i) {   mk_pop_gen(doses[i]) }) names(population_generators) <- arm_names

title: Example 5: Multi-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Dunnett test + MCP-Mod
text: final time, run: Dunnett test (active vs placebo) using multcomp::glht lm(y ~ arm). MCP-Mod using DoseFinding::MCPMod candidate model set (Mods). Dunnett test (multcomp::glht mcp(arm = "Dunnett")) simultaneously compares active dose placebo controlling family-wise error rate alpha. MCP-Mod run five candidate dose-response shapes; selModel = "aveAIC" selects best model AIC-weighted averaging, Delta = delta sets minimum effect size clinical relevance contrast step. mct_min_p extracts minimum p-value across candidate model contrast tests — small value indicates least one candidate model detects dose-response signal.
code: # Candidate model set for MCP-Mod mods <- Mods(   linear     = NULL,   emax       = 20,   exponential= 50,   sigEmax    = c(20, 3),   quadratic  = -0.2,   doses      = doses )  analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer){       df_e <- df |>         dplyr::filter(!is.na(enroll_time)) |>         dplyr::mutate(           arm  = factor(arm, levels = paste0("d", doses)),           dose = as.numeric(dose)         )        # 1) Dunnett (active vs placebo)       fit <- lm(y ~ arm, data = df_e)       dun  <- multcomp::glht(fit, linfct = multcomp::mcp(arm = "Dunnett"))       summ <- summary(dun)        # 2) MCP-Mod (one-step)       mm <- DoseFinding::MCPMod(           dose   = df_e$dose,           resp   = df_e$y,           models = mods,           type   = "normal",           Delta  = delta,           alpha  = alpha,           selModel = "aveAIC"       )        mct_min_p <- min(attr(mm$MCTtest$tStat, "pVal"), na.rm = TRUE)        data.frame(         scenario,         n_total      = nrow(df_e),         dunn_min_p   = summ$test$pvalues |> as.numeric() |> min(),         mcpmod_min_p = mct_min_p,         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 6: Two-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Overall + exploratory subgroup analysis
text: two-arm, fixed design trial (placebo vs treatment) single continuous endpoint one final analysis. final analysis, report: Overall analysis: treatment effect across subjects. Exploratory subgroup analysis: treatment effect within subgroup + arm--subgroup interaction test. Treatment effect heterogeneity arises patients different baseline characteristics — disease subtype biomarker status — respond differently treatment. , delta_A = 0.40 delta_B = 0.15 encode larger benefit subgroup ; treatment effective subgroups, considerably . subgroup_prob = c(0.6, 0.4) means average 60% enrolled patients belong subgroup 40% subgroup B, though actual proportions vary across replicates due random assignment.
code: # Trial design sample_size <- 120 allocation  <- c(1, 1) arms        <- c("placebo", "treatment")  # Subgroup settings subgroup_levels <- c("A", "B") subgroup_prob   <- c(0.6, 0.4)  # Data-generating truth (example values) mu_placebo_A <- 0.00 mu_placebo_B <- 0.00 delta_A      <- 0.40  # true treatment effect in subgroup A delta_B      <- 0.15  # true treatment effect in subgroup B sigma        <- 1.0  # Enrollment/dropout profile enrollment <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8),   rate     = c(8, 7) ) dropout <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8),   rate     = c(0, 0) )  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size  = sample_size,   allocation   = list(allocation),   delta_A      = delta_A,   delta_B      = delta_B,   p_subgroup_A = subgroup_prob[1],   p_subgroup_B = subgroup_prob[2] )

text: Create two populations include subgroup column population dataset. generator, subgroup membership randomly drawn subject probabilities c(0.6, 0.4), arm-specific mean selected conditional assignment. subgroup sampled independently replicate, realized subgroup counts — hence per-subgroup sample sizes — vary across replicates, reflecting realistic pre-stratified (block-randomised) design.
code: # Create generator for arm-specific data with subgroups population_generators <- list(   placebo = function(n) {     subgroup <- sample(subgroup_levels, size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = subgroup_prob)     mu <- ifelse(subgroup == "A", mu_placebo_A, mu_placebo_B)     data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       subgroup = subgroup,       y = rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma),       readout_time = 1     )   },   treatment = function(n) {     subgroup <- sample(subgroup_levels, size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = subgroup_prob)     mu <- ifelse(subgroup == "A", mu_placebo_A + delta_A, mu_placebo_B + delta_B)     data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       subgroup = subgroup,       y = rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma),       readout_time = 1     )   } )

title: Example 6: Two-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Overall + exploratory subgroup analysis
text: lm(y ~ arm) estimates overall treatment effect across subjects. Per-subgroup effects obtained fitting lm(y ~ arm) separately within subgroup subset. interaction model lm(y ~ arm * subgroup) includes arm:subgroupB coefficient tests directly whether treatment effect subgroup B differs reference subgroup — statistically significant result indicates genuine treatment effect heterogeneity.
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       dat <- df |>         dplyr::filter(!is.na(enroll_time)) |>         dplyr::mutate(           arm = factor(arm, levels = c("placebo", "treatment")),           subgroup = factor(subgroup, levels = subgroup_levels)         )        fit_overall <- lm(y ~ arm, data = dat)       coef_overall <- summary(fit_overall)$coefficients        overall_est <- unname(coef_overall["armtreatment", "Estimate"])       overall_p   <- unname(coef_overall["armtreatment", "Pr(>|t|)"])        subgroup_stats <- lapply(levels(dat$subgroup), function(sg) {         dsg <- dat[dat$subgroup == sg, , drop = FALSE]         fit_sg <- lm(y ~ arm, data = dsg)         coef_sg <- summary(fit_sg)$coefficients          c(           n_total = nrow(dsg),           estimate = unname(coef_sg["armtreatment", "Estimate"]),           p_value = unname(coef_sg["armtreatment", "Pr(>|t|)"])         )       })       names(subgroup_stats) <- levels(dat$subgroup)        fit_interaction <- lm(y ~ arm * subgroup, data = dat)       coef_int <- summary(fit_interaction)$coefficients       int_term <- grep("^arm.*:subgroup", rownames(coef_int), value = TRUE)       p_interaction <- if (length(int_term) > 0) {         unname(coef_int[int_term[1], "Pr(>|t|)"])       } else {         NA_real_       }        data.frame(         scenario,         n_total = nrow(dat),         overall_estimate = overall_est,         overall_p_value = overall_p,         subgroup_A_n = unname(subgroup_stats[["A"]]["n_total"]),         subgroup_A_estimate = unname(subgroup_stats[["A"]]["estimate"]),         subgroup_A_p_value = unname(subgroup_stats[["A"]]["p_value"]),         subgroup_B_n = unname(subgroup_stats[["B"]]["n_total"]),         subgroup_B_estimate = unname(subgroup_stats[["B"]]["estimate"]),         subgroup_B_p_value = unname(subgroup_stats[["B"]]["p_value"]),         interaction_p_value = p_interaction,         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 7: Two-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go with placebo borrowing
text: two-arm, fixed design trial (placebo vs treatment) single continuous endpoint single final analysis. Placebo arm uses historical borrowing informative prior derived historical placebo data robustified non-informative component. Treatment arm uses non-informative prior. Go/-Go rule final analysis: Go P(Δ>δ∣data)≥γ, \text{Go } P(\Delta > \delta \mid \text{data}) \ge \gamma, Δ=μT−μP\Delta = \mu_T - \mu_P, δ=0.1\delta = 0.1 γ=0.8\gamma = 0.8. delta = 0.1 defines minimum treatment-placebo difference clinical relevance — effect must exceed threshold drug worth advancing. gamma = 0.8 requires 80% posterior probability exceeding threshold Go decision issued, balancing false-positive risk cost missed opportunity. true simulated effect mu_treat = 0.30 lies delta, well-powered trial frequently result Go.
code: # Trial design sample_size <- 80 allocation  <- c(1, 1) arms        <- c("placebo", "treatment")  # Data-generating truth for this simulation example mu_placebo_true <- 0.00 delta_true      <- 0.30  # true treatment - placebo mean difference sigma_known     <- 1.0  # Enrollment/dropout profile (fixed design timeline) enrollment <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8),   rate     = c(6, 10) ) dropout <- list(   end_time = c(4, 8),   rate     = c(0, 0) )  # Bayesian decision thresholds delta <- 0.1 gamma <- 0.8  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation  = list(allocation),   delta_true  = delta_true,   delta       = delta,   gamma       = gamma )

title: Example 7: Two-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go with placebo borrowing
text: include small historical placebo dataset study-level means sample sizes. Studies H1, H2, H3 contribute combined 62 historical placebo observations summarised informative prior via postmix() — Bayesian update non-informative starting prior pooled historical data. robustify() blends informative component vague component (20% weight), producing mixture prior protects prior-data conflict: current trial’s placebo behaves unexpectedly, vague component limits strongly historical data pulls posterior. treatment arm uses prior_noninf (effectively flat prior), treatment posterior driven entirely current data.
code: hist_placebo <- data.frame(   study = c("H1", "H2", "H3"),   n     = c(24, 18, 20),   mean  = c(-0.05, 0.02, 0.00) )  hist_placebo #>   study  n  mean #> 1    H1 24 -0.05 #> 2    H2 18  0.02 #> 3    H3 20  0.00  # Pooled historical summary used to derive an informative placebo prior n_hist_total <- sum(hist_placebo$n) m_hist_pooled <- weighted.mean(hist_placebo$mean, hist_placebo$n)  # Non-informative prior (very small pseudo-sample size in mn parametrization) # Used as treatment prior and as base for historical update. prior_noninf <- mixnorm(   c(1, 0, 1e-6),   sigma = sigma_known,   param = "mn" )  # Informative placebo prior from historical placebo summary prior_placebo_inf <- postmix(   prior_noninf,   n = n_hist_total,   m = m_hist_pooled ) #> Using default prior reference scale 1  # Robustified placebo prior to protect against prior-data conflict prior_placebo <- robustify(   prior_placebo_inf,   weight = 0.2,   mean   = 0,   n      = 1,   sigma  = sigma_known )  # Treatment prior remains non-informative prior_treatment <- prior_noninf  summary(prior_placebo) #>        mean          sd        2.5%       50.0%       97.5%  #> -0.01083871  0.46144620 -1.15034971 -0.01312827  1.15034975

title: Example 7: Two-arm | Fixed design | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go with placebo borrowing
text: one trigger added final analysis. RBesT::pmixdiff(post_treat, post_placebo, delta, lower.tail = FALSE) evaluates P(μ_T − μ_P > δ | data) numerical integration mixture posterior distributions treatment placebo. qmixdiff() computes quantiles posterior difference distribution, yielding 95% credible interval Δ = μ_T − μ_P.
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       dat <- df |>         dplyr::filter(!is.na(enroll_time)) |>         dplyr::mutate(arm = factor(arm, levels = c("placebo", "treatment")))        y_p <- dat$y[dat$arm == "placebo"]       y_t <- dat$y[dat$arm == "treatment"]        n_p <- length(y_p)       n_t <- length(y_t)        m_p <- mean(y_p)       m_t <- mean(y_t)        # Posterior for each arm mean       post_placebo <- RBesT::postmix(prior_placebo, n = n_p, m = m_p)       post_treat   <- RBesT::postmix(prior_treatment, n = n_t, m = m_t)        # Posterior probability for treatment effect exceeding delta       prob_delta <- RBesT::pmixdiff(post_treat, post_placebo, delta, lower.tail = FALSE)        # Posterior summaries for Delta = mu_T - mu_P       delta_ci <- RBesT::qmixdiff(post_treat, post_placebo, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))        data.frame(         scenario,         n_placebo = n_p,         n_treatment = n_t,         mean_placebo = m_p,         mean_treatment = m_t,         post_prob_delta = prob_delta,         delta_q025 = delta_ci[1],         delta_q500 = delta_ci[2],         delta_q975 = delta_ci[3],         decision_go = as.integer(prob_delta >= gamma),         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Example 8: Seamless Ph2a/2b | Two-arm -> Multi-arm | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go + Bayesian MCP-Mod
text: seamless Ph2a/2b trial one continuous endpoint: Ph2a (early period): 2 arms (placebo + one active dose). Ph2b (later period): expansion multi-arm dose finding. Interim: Bayesian Go/-Go based treatment effect Ph2a dose. Final: Bayesian MCP-Mod across doses. dose_map defines five arms: d0 (placebo, 0 mg), d5 (5 mg), d10 (10 mg), d20 (20 mg), d40 (40 mg). Emax truth emax = 0.9 ed50 = 15, meaning dose-response curve saturates around 30–40 mg. interim Go/-Go time 4 uses delta_interim = 0.1 gamma_interim = 0.8 (convention Example 7), alpha_final = 0.05 controls Type error final Bayesian MCP-Mod step.
code: # Dose levels and arm labels dose_map <- c(d0 = 0, d5 = 5, d10 = 10, d20 = 20, d40 = 40) arm_names <- names(dose_map) dose_levels <- as.numeric(dose_map)  # Truth for data generation (Emax shape) e0    <- 0.0 emax  <- 0.9 ed50  <- 15 sigma <- 1.0 mean_fun <- function(d) e0 + emax * d / (ed50 + d)  # Interim Go/No-Go thresholds delta_interim <- 0.1 gamma_interim <- 0.8  # Multiplicity level for final Bayesian MCP step alpha_final <- 0.05  # Planned analysis times interim_time <- 4 final_time   <- 12

title: Example 8: Seamless Ph2a/2b | Two-arm -> Multi-arm | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go + Bayesian MCP-Mod
text: implementation, arms pre-defined, enrollment switches two-arm multi-arm time. enroll_plan data frame specifies integer subject counts (enroller) per arm per time unit. time 1–4 (Ph2a), d0 d20 enroll (8 per time ). time 5 onward (Ph2b), three new doses (d5, d10, d40) begin enrolling d20 arm tapers ; d0 continues 8 per time throughout. dropper set 0 (dropout). tr_timer$get_end_timepoint() confirms last time point enrollment occurs.
code: time <- 1:12  # Ph2a: only placebo + d20 active # Ph2b: expand to d5, d10, d40 while placebo/d20 continue per plan enroll_plan <- data.frame(   time = rep(time, times = length(arm_names)),   arm  = rep(arm_names, each = length(time)),   enroller = c(     # d0 placebo (total 80)     8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 0, 0,     # d5 (total 20)     0, 0, 0, 0, 3, 3, 4, 3, 3, 4, 0, 0,     # d10 (total 20)     0, 0, 0, 0, 3, 3, 4, 3, 3, 4, 0, 0,     # d20 (total 40)     8, 8, 8, 8, 2, 2, 2, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0,     # d40 (total 20)     0, 0, 0, 0, 3, 3, 4, 3, 3, 4, 0, 0   ),   dropper = 0L )  enroll_plan$enroller <- as.integer(enroll_plan$enroller) enroll_plan$dropper  <- as.integer(enroll_plan$dropper)  tr_timer <- Timer$new(name = "timer_example_8") add_timepoints(tr_timer, enroll_plan)  tr_timer$get_end_timepoint() #> [1] 12

title: Example 8: Seamless Ph2a/2b | Two-arm -> Multi-arm | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go + Bayesian MCP-Mod
text: mk_pop() creates Population objects directly pre-generated data (generator functions), computing arm’s n sum(enroller) enrollment plan. approach pre-generates full cohort -front; Trial$new() draws population’s pool according time-specific enrollment counts.
code: # Planned max N by arm from enrollment schedule n_by_arm <- enroll_plan |>   group_by(arm) |>   summarise(n = sum(enroller), .groups = "drop")  mk_pop <- function(arm, n) {   d <- dose_map[[arm]]   Population$new(     name = arm,     data = data.frame(       id = seq_len(n),       dose = d,       y = rnorm(n, mean = mean_fun(d), sd = sigma),       readout_time = 1     )   ) }  pops <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(n_by_arm)), function(i) {   mk_pop(n_by_arm$arm[i], n_by_arm$n[i]) })

title: Example 8: Seamless Ph2a/2b | Two-arm -> Multi-arm | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go + Bayesian MCP-Mod
text: interim Go/-Go, use placebo borrowing small historical placebo data non-informative prior active. final Bayesian MCP-Mod, pass arm-wise priors BayesianMCPMod. historical borrowing approach Example 7 used interim final placebo priors. prior_list_final five entries — Ctrl (robustified historical placebo prior) DG_1 DG_4 (non-informative priors d5, d10, d20, d40) — matching five dose groups expected BayesianMCPMod::assessDesign().
code: # Historical placebo summary data hist_placebo <- data.frame(   study = c("H1", "H2", "H3"),   n     = c(20, 24, 18),   mean  = c(-0.06, 0.00, 0.03) )  n_hist <- sum(hist_placebo$n) m_hist <- weighted.mean(hist_placebo$mean, hist_placebo$n)  # Base non-informative prior prior_noninf <- RBesT::mixnorm(c(1, 0, 1e-6), sigma = sigma, param = "mn")  # Placebo borrowing prior for interim and final control arm prior_placebo_inf <- RBesT::postmix(prior_noninf, n = n_hist, m = m_hist) #> Using default prior reference scale 1 prior_placebo <- RBesT::robustify(   prior_placebo_inf,   weight = 0.2,   mean = 0,   n = 1,   sigma = sigma )  # Prior list for Bayesian MCP-Mod (Ctrl + 4 active groups) prior_list_final <- list(   Ctrl = prior_placebo,   DG_1 = prior_noninf,   DG_2 = prior_noninf,   DG_3 = prior_noninf,   DG_4 = prior_noninf )

title: Example 8: Seamless Ph2a/2b | Two-arm -> Multi-arm | Single continuous endpoint | Bayesian Go/No-Go + Bayesian MCP-Mod
text: add two trigger conditions: time == interim_time: Bayesian Go/-Go d20 vs placebo. time == final_time: Bayesian MCP-Mod dose groups. trigger_by_calendar(interim_time, tr_timer, ...) registers interim analysis fire calendar time reaches interim_time = 4. <<- operator go_interim <<- .integer(...) writes interim Go/-Go decision enclosing environment, making available downstream analyses conditional logic final step. final analysis calls BayesianMCPMod::assessDesign() arm-level sample sizes (n_patients), candidate dose-response models (mods), five-arm prior list (prior_list), arm-level posterior mean estimates (estimates_sim); alpha_crit_val = alpha_final sets significance threshold Bayesian MCP contrast step.
code: # shared state between interim and final trigger go_interim <- NA_integer_  # Candidate dose-response models for Bayesian MCP-Mod mods <- DoseFinding::Mods(   linear      = NULL,   emax        = 20,   exponential = 20,   sigEmax     = c(20, 3),   doses       = dose_levels )  # Interim: Bayesian Go/No-Go (d20 vs placebo) trigger_by_calendar(interim_time, tr_timer, analysis = function(df, current_time) {   dat <- df |>     dplyr::filter(!is.na(enroll_time), arm %in% c("d0", "d20"))    y_p <- dat$y[dat$arm == "d0"]   y_t <- dat$y[dat$arm == "d20"]    n_p <- length(y_p)   n_t <- length(y_t)   m_p <- mean(y_p)   m_t <- mean(y_t)    post_p <- RBesT::postmix(prior_placebo, n = n_p, m = m_p)   post_t <- RBesT::postmix(prior_noninf,  n = n_t, m = m_t)    prob_delta <- RBesT::pmixdiff(post_t, post_p, delta_interim, lower.tail = FALSE)   delta_q <- RBesT::qmixdiff(post_t, post_p, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))    go_interim <<- as.integer(prob_delta >= gamma_interim)    list(     n_placebo = n_p,     n_d20 = n_t,     post_prob_delta = prob_delta,     delta_q025 = delta_q[1],     delta_q500 = delta_q[2],     delta_q975 = delta_q[3],     decision_go = go_interim   ) })  # Final: Bayesian MCP-Mod across all dose groups tr_timer$add_condition(   time == final_time,   analysis = function(df, time) {     dat <- df |>       dplyr::filter(!is.na(enroll_time))      # Arm-wise summary at final     summ <- dat |>       dplyr::group_by(arm) |>       dplyr::summarise(         dose = unique(dose)[1],         n = dplyr::n(),         mu_hat = mean(y),         .groups = "drop"       ) |>       dplyr::arrange(dose)      # Order expected by BayesianMCPMod: Ctrl, DG_1 ...     # with dose order 0, 5, 10, 20, 40.     n_vec <- summ$n |> as.double()     mu_vec <- summ$mu_hat      # covariance of mean estimates (independent groups here)     S_hat <- diag((sigma / sqrt(n_vec))^2)      # Prepare estimates from observed data for assessDesign     estimates_sim <- list(       mu_hats = list(mu_vec),       S_hats = list(S_hat)     )      # Run Bayesian MCP-Mod assessment with observed data     bmcp <- BayesianMCPMod::assessDesign(       n_patients = n_vec,       mods = mods,       prior_list = prior_list_final,       estimates_sim = estimates_sim,       alpha_crit_val = alpha_final,       n_sim = 1     )      # Extract key results into row-bindable format     data.frame(       n_total_final = nrow(dat),       max_eff = attr(bmcp, "maxEff")     )   },   name = "final_bayesian_mcpmod" )

title: Getting Started
code: library(rxsim)

title: Getting Started
text: scenario tibble captures design parameters want track results — sample size, allocation ratios, factors might vary across simulation runs. Wrapping tidyr::expand_grid() produces one-row data frame gets embedded every analysis result. later stack results across many scenarios sensitivity analyses, metadata keeps row traceable back design.
code: sample_size <- 30 arms        <- c("control", "treatment") allocation  <- c(1, 1) true_delta  <- 0.5  scenario <- tidyr::expand_grid(   sample_size = sample_size,   allocation  = list(allocation),   true_delta  = true_delta )

title: Getting Started
text: trial arm represented generator function — plain R function takes n (number subjects planned arm) returns data frame subject-level endpoint data. rxsim calls functions per replicate draw fresh population, every replicate gets independent data. data frame must contain least three columns: id — unique integer identifier per subject readout_time — time (trial-time units) enrollment endpoint observed (use 0 baseline immediately-available data) least one endpoint column vector_to_dataframe() convenience helper wraps numeric vector standard format id, data, readout_time = 0. simulate simple continuous endpoint: control arm standard-normal, treatment arm mean shift true_delta.
code: population_generators <- list(   control   = function(n) vector_to_dataframe(rnorm(n)),   treatment = function(n) vector_to_dataframe(rnorm(n, mean = true_delta)) )

title: Getting Started
text: Enrollment dropout modelled random processes. function takes n returns vector inter-event times — waiting times successive enrollments (dropouts). times drawn independently every replicate, giving trial enrollment trajectory. rexp(n, rate = 1) generates exponentially-distributed inter-arrival times mean gap 1 time unit enrolments — common approximation Poisson arrivals. lower dropout rate (0.05) reflects trial subjects complete study.
code: enrollment <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1.0) dropout    <- function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.05)

title: Getting Started
text: Analysis triggers heart rxsim. trigger pairs condition (fire) analysis function (compute fires). condition written dplyr-style boolean expression inside rlang::exprs(). evaluated snapshot currently enrolled subjects timepoint. condition fires full enrollment reached, .e., sample_size subjects accumulated non-NA enroll_time. !! operator (pronounced “bang-bang”) injects current value sample_size expression definition time, rather looking evaluation time. necessary expression stored evaluated later inside simulation loop. condition met, rxsim calls analysis function two arguments: df: data frame snapshot enrolled subjects triggering timepoint, columns population data plus enroll_time, drop_time, arm time: current trial clock time trigger fired function return data frame (one row per trigger event conventional pattern).
code: analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger = rlang::exprs(       sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size     ),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))       data.frame(         scenario,         n_enrolled  = nrow(enrolled),         mean_ctrl   = mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "control"]),         mean_trt    = mean(enrolled$data[enrolled$arm == "treatment"]),         stringsAsFactors = FALSE       )     }   ) )

title: Getting Started

code: set.seed(42)  trials <- replicate_trial(   trial_name            = "getting_started",   sample_size           = sample_size,   arms                  = arms,   allocation            = allocation,   enrollment            = enrollment,   dropout               = dropout,   analysis_generators   = analysis_generators,   population_generators = population_generators,   n                     = 5 ) title: Reducing friction for Randomized Clinical Trial simulations
text: Simulating randomized controlled trial scratch means manually wiring together enrollment schedules, randomization, data snapshots, analyses, result collection — across potentially thousands replicates. Done base R even tidyverse, orchestration routinely reaches 1,000+ lines fragile, hard--reuse, -maintain, -review code. rxsim replaces scaffolding <100 lines high-level, declarative code, can stay focused design decisions matter: endpoints, allocation, analysis rules, adaptive strategies.

title: Reducing friction for Randomized Clinical Trial simulations

title: Reducing friction for Randomized Clinical Trial simulations
text: two-arm, fixed-design trial continuous endpoint 30 lines: Collect results across replicates one pass — see Getting Started vignette full walkthrough.
code: library(rxsim)  # 1. Define populations — one generator function per arm population_generators <- list(   placebo   = function(n) data.frame(id = 1:n, y = rnorm(n, 0.0), readout_time = 1),   treatment = function(n) data.frame(id = 1:n, y = rnorm(n, 0.3), readout_time = 1) )  # 2. Define what triggers an analysis and what to compute sample_size <- 100L analysis_generators <- list(   final = list(     trigger  = rlang::exprs(sum(!is.na(enroll_time)) >= !!sample_size),     analysis = function(df, timer) {       enrolled <- subset(df, !is.na(enroll_time))       data.frame(n = nrow(enrolled), p_value = t.test(y ~ arm, data = enrolled)$p.value)     }   ) )  # 3. Create 500 independent replicates and run them all trials <- replicate_trial(   trial_name            = "phase2_example",   sample_size           = sample_size,   arms                  = c("placebo", "treatment"),   allocation            = c(1, 1),   enrollment            = function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1),   dropout               = function(n) rexp(n, rate = 0.01),   analysis_generators   = analysis_generators,   population_generators = population_generators,   n                     = 500 )  run_trials(trials)

title: Reducing friction for Randomized Clinical Trial simulations
text: Building clinical trial simulation scratch typically balloons 1,000+ lines low-level code. Even tidyverse, orchestration entirely : write clock loop, decide analyses fire, manage state across replicates, handle result aggregation. rxsim provides orchestration layer box. simulation fits <100 lines high-level, readable code section clear purpose: define trial scenario, populations, analysis rules, replicate, run. dramatically reduces cognitive burden makes faster : - prototype new trial design - swap endpoint model - add/remove arms - add/remove looks analyses - hand code colleague  . rxsim supports number arms, endpoint type, arbitrary analysis functions. built-examples cover continuous endpoints, correlated multi-endpoints, time--event endpoints, multi-arm dose-finding (Dunnett + MCP-Mod), subgroup analyses, Bayesian Go/-Go rules historical borrowing. analysis can written R function, rxsim can run .  Yes. Trigger conditions fire whenever boolean expression accumulating data satisfied — calendar time, enrollment fraction, custom condition. Multiple triggers can registered trial, allowing interim final analyses coexist naturally. Adaptive rules (e.g., stopping early, adjusting sample size) can implemented inside analysis functions propagated across trial state. See Core Concepts detailed explanation trigger system.  rxsim designed rapid trial design prototyping operating characteristic evaluation — estimating power, type error, decision probabilities range scenarios. validated submission package, yet. Simulation outputs produces can feed submission-supporting analyses user applies appropriate validation documentation.  replicate_trial(n = N) creates N fully independent Trial objects (population data timer state). run_trials() executes sequentially. large N, parallelization straightforward — replace run_trials() R parallel back-end:  functions produce enrollment/dropout schedule feeds Timer, model timing differently: gen_plan() stochastic: supply inter-event time probability distribution function (e.g., function(n) rexp(n, rate = 1)). replicate gets different realization enrollment process, capturing natural variability study timelines. gen_timepoints() deterministic piecewise-constant rate: specify enrollment rates per time period (e.g., 5 patients/month months 1–4, 10/month). Every replicate follows fixed schedule. See Enrollment & Dropout Modeling vignette side--side comparison.
